text: We are reporting a left lower baslobectomy for bronchiectasis. The CT scan depicted an extensive involvement of the left lower lobe by the process. It has been provoking several episodes of pneumonia over the years to the patient.
Using the triportal Copenhagen approach, the thoracoscopy reveals stiff adhesions between the involved lobe, diaphragm, and mediastinum.
The surgeon uses a high-energy device to dissect adhesions, achieving effective hemostasis and preventing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the fissure to dissect the branches of the pulmonary artery to the lower lobe, with enlarged lymph nodes visibly surrounding the vessels.

The surgeon identifies a markedly enlarged bronchial artery in the posterior aspect of the pulmonary hilum, located behind the apical segmental artery. The vessel is then sutured using a stapling device after completing the section.
The surgeon isolates a pathological bronchial artery within the pulmonary ligament and closes it using a high energy device, facilitating the safe isolation and ligation of the lower pulmonary vein.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the fissure and proceeds to close the bronchus, using surgical instruments to secure the bronchial closure.
The surgeon extracts the specimen using an endo bag. Following the extraction, a single chest tube is inserted through the posterior axis to facilitate drainage.
